title: How the claustrum integrates sensory information during dissociation
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The claustrum is a thin sheet of neurons buried deep in your brain.

Most people have never heard of it. I hadn't either, until a few years ago when I started digging into what actually happens in the nervous system during dissociation. You know that feeling when the world goes far away, like you're watching your life through a dirty window? That's not just a metaphor. Something physical is happening inside your skull.

The claustrum sits between the insula and the putamen. It's about the thickness of a credit card. For decades, neuroscientists thought it was mostly a relay station, just passing signals from one part of the brain to another. But recent research suggests something much stranger. This tiny structure might be the conductor of your conscious experience. And when it gets overwhelmed, when trauma floods the system, the claustrum may help the very thing we call dissociation.

Stay with me here. I know this sounds technical. But I promise this matters for anyone who has ever felt their body go numb during a triggering conversation, or watched themselves from above during a panic attack, or simply checked out during sex when their mind couldn't handle what was happening.

Dissociation isn't a character flaw. It's a survival strategy. And the claustrum might be one of its key architects.

What the claustrum actually does

Think of the claustrum as a kind of sensory traffic controller. Every second, your brain receives an enormous amount of information. Light hitting your retinas. Sound waves hitting your eardrums. Pressure on your skin. Your internal organs sending signals about hunger, fullness, pain, heart rate. It's too much. Way too much. Your brain can't process all of it consciously.

So it filters. It prioritizes. It decides what matters right now and pushes everything else into the background. That's the claustrum's job. It integrates information from different sensory systems and helps create a unified experience of the present moment.

Francis Crick, the same guy who co-discovered DNA, spent his later years obsessed with the claustrum. He believed it was essential for consciousness itself. He and his colleague Christof Koch proposed that the claustrum might bind together different sensory streams into one coherent experience. The color red, the smell of rain, the feeling of your feet on the ground, the sound of your own breath. All of that gets woven together into a single moment. And the claustrum might be the loom.

Here's the thing. If the claustrum is damaged, consciousness fragments. People with claustrum lesions have reported feeling like they're in a dream, or that parts of their experience don't belong to them. Sound familiar? That's dissociation.

Now consider what happens during trauma. The nervous system gets flooded. The brain detects threat. And suddenly, integration becomes dangerous. If you fully felt what was happening to you, it might destroy you. So the brain does something . It pulls the plug on integration. It lets sensory information stay separate, unbound, fragmented.

Wild, right? The same structure that normally creates your sense of being a unified self might also be the one that helps you fall apart when you need to survive.

How dissociation changes sensory processing

I remember sitting in my therapist's office years ago, describing how I could see her mouth moving but couldn't hear the words. Or how my own hands looked like they belonged to someone else. She nodded and said, "That's dissociation." But nobody explained the mechanics. Nobody told me there was a physical process happening in my brain. I thought I was broken.

You're not broken. Your brain was doing exactly what it evolved to do. When threat exceeds your capacity to cope, the claustrum may reduce its integrative function. Sensory information stops getting bound together. Your visual experience separates from your auditory experience. Your body sensations detach from your emotional state. Your sense of self floats away from your physical location.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma lives in the body. He talks about how animals in the wild shake off the effects of threat once the danger passes. But humans get stuck. We freeze. We dissociate. And the sensory integration that should happen naturally gets disrupted.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored by Deb Dana. She makes the polyvagal framework accessible. She shows how the vagus nerve, the claustrum's partner in many ways, regulates our ability to stay present or check out.

When dissociation becomes chronic, the claustrum might get stuck in a pattern. It learns to keep sensory streams separate as a default. Your brain forgets how to integrate. You walk around feeling like a ghost in your own life. You're not really here. You're not really anywhere.

This is why grounding techniques work, sometimes. They force the claustrum to pay attention to one sensory stream at a time. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice the texture of your shirt. Look at three things in the room. You're retraining the integrative process. You're saying to your brain, "It's safe to bind this information together now."

The polyvagal connection

Stephen Porges developed polyvagal theory, and his work changed how I understand my own body. He showed that the vagus nerve has two branches. The ventral vagal branch is connected to social engagement, safety, and connection. The dorsal vagal branch is older, more primitive. It's responsible for shutdown, collapse, and dissociation.

Here's where it gets interesting. The claustrum receives input from the vagus nerve. When your nervous system detects danger, the dorsal vagal branch activates. Your heart rate drops. Your breathing slows. Your awareness narrows. And the claustrum may respond by reducing its integrative function. It's like the brain says, "We're in survival mode. We don't need a rich, unified experience. We just need to get through this."

So dissociation isn't just a psychological response. It's a whole-body, whole-brain survival strategy coordinated by multiple systems working together. The vagus nerve signals danger. The claustrum responds by fragmenting experience. And you end up floating above yourself, watching from a distance, feeling nothing.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana. It gives you exercises and maps for understanding your own nervous system states. I've used it with clients and in my own recovery.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you just take a shallow breath? Did your shoulders tighten? Did you feel a familiar distance creeping in? That's your claustrum and vagus nerve talking to each other. They're asking, "Is it safe to be here?"

Recovery means teaching the claustrum to integrate again

I don't believe in quick fixes. I don't think you can meditate your way out of complex trauma in thirty days. But I do believe the brain has neuroplasticity. I believe the claustrum can learn new patterns. It can remember how to bind sensory information together, even after years of dissociation.

The process is slow. It's messy. It involves titration, pendulation, and a lot of patience. You have to expose yourself to small doses of integration. Let yourself feel your feet on the floor for thirty seconds. Then stop. Then do it again tomorrow. You're building a new neural pathway. You're telling the claustrum, "This is safe. You can let the information come together now."

Janina Fisher, who wrote about trauma-informed stabilization treatment, talks about how the traumatized parts of us need to be witnessed, not pushed away. The dissociated parts need to be acknowledged. When you feel that floaty sensation, instead of fighting it, you can say, "Oh, there's that part of me that's checking out. I see you. You helped me survive. But right now, I'm safe."

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It shifts the question from "What's wrong with you?" to "What happened to you?" That small change matters. It changes how you relate to your own dissociative experiences.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine. It was one of the first books that helped me understand why my body kept doing things my mind didn't want it to do. Levine's work on the freeze response and how to complete thwarted defensive movements is directly relevant to the claustrum's role in dissociation.

I'm not saying recovery is linear. It's not. You'll have days where you feel fully present, where the world feels real and your body feels like yours. And then you'll have days where the dissociation comes back like a wave, and you're floating again. That's normal. That's the nervous system doing its job. The claustrum is still learning. It's still testing the waters.

But over time, the waves get smaller. The present moments get longer. The window of tolerance expands. And one day you realize you've been sitting with yourself for an hour, fully present, without checking out. And that's not nothing. That's everything.

I still dissociate sometimes. I'm not cured. But I understand now that my claustrum was trying to protect me. It was doing its job too well. And now I'm teaching it a new job. Integration. Presence. Being here, in this body, in this moment, even when it's hard.

Sit with that.
